functions.name: vaex.jupyter.utils.flush
documentation:
Run all non-executed debounced functions.

If execution of debounced calls lead to scheduling of new calls, they will be recursively executed,
with a limit or recursive_counts calls. recursive_counts=-1 means infinite.

functions.name: vaex.jupyter.utils.gather
documentation: Gather all debounced function result, useful for waiting till all schedules operations are executed

functions.name: vaex.jupyter.utils.kernel_tick
documentation: Execute a single command, to allow events from the frontend to get to the kernel during execution.

functions.name: vaex.jupyter.utils.debounced
documentation:
A decorator to debounce many method/function call into 1 call.

Note: this only works in an async environment, such as a Jupyter notebook context. Outside
of this context, calling {func}`flush` will execute pending calls.

```
param float delay_seconds

The amount of seconds that should pass without any call, before the (final) call will be executed.

param bool method

The decorator should know if the callable is a a method or not, otherwise the debounced is on a per-class basis.

param bool skip_gather

The decorated function will be be waited for when calling vaex.jupyter.gather()

param on_error

callback function that takes an exception as argument.

param bool reentrant

reentrant function or not
```

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.DataFrameAccessorWidget.data_array
documentation:
Create a {func}`vaex.jupyter.model.DataArray` model and {func}`vaex.jupyter.view.DataArray` widget and links them.

This is a convenience method to create the model and view, and hook them up.

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.DataFrameAccessorWidget.expression
documentation:
Create a widget to edit a vaex expression.

If value is an {class}`vaex.jupyter.model.Axis` object, its expression will be (bi-directionally) linked to the widget.

```
param value

Valid expression (string or Expression object), or Axis
```

inner_classes.name: vaex.jupyter.model.Axis.Status
documentation:
State transitions
NO_LIMITS -> STAGED_CALCULATING_LIMITS -> CALCULATING_LIMITS -> CALCULATED_LIMITS -> READY

when expression changes:
    STAGED_CALCULATING_LIMITS: 
        calculation.cancel()
        ->NO_LIMITS
    CALCULATING_LIMITS: 
        calculation.cancel()
        ->NO_LIMITS

when min/max changes:
    STAGED_CALCULATING_LIMITS: 
        calculation.cancel()
        ->NO_LIMITS
    CALCULATING_LIMITS: 
        calculation.cancel()
        ->NO_LIMITS

documentation: def model_remove(self, model, regrid=True):
index = self.models.index(model)
del self.models[index]
del self._callbacks_regrid[index]
del self._callbacks_slice[index]

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.model.GridCalculator.reslice
documentation: @vaex.jupyter.debounced(delay_seconds=0.05, reentrant=False)
def reslice_debounced(self):
self.reslice()
A grid is responsible for scheduling the grid calculations and possible slicing

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.view.DataArray
documentation:
Will display a DataArray interactively, with an optional custom display_function.

By default, it will simply display(...) the DataArray, using xarray's default display mechanism.

functions.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.contextmanager
documentation:
@contextmanager decorator.

Typical usage:

    @contextmanager
    def some_generator(<arguments>):
        <setup>
        try:
            yield <value>
        finally:
            <cleanup>

This makes this:

    with some_generator(<arguments>) as <variable>:
        <body>

equivalent to this:

    <setup>
    try:
        <variable> = <value>
        <body>
    finally:
        <cleanup>

functions.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.asynccontextmanager
documentation:
@asynccontextmanager decorator.

Typical usage:

    @asynccontextmanager
    async def some_async_generator(<arguments>):
        <setup>
        try:
            yield <value>
        finally:
            <cleanup>

This makes this:

    async with some_async_generator(<arguments>) as <variable>:
        <body>

equivalent to this:

    <setup>
    try:
        <variable> = <value>
        <body>
    finally:
        <cleanup>

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.ContextDecorator._recreate_cm
documentation:
Return a recreated instance of self.

Allows an otherwise one-shot context manager like
_GeneratorContextManager to support use as
a decorator via implicit recreation.

This is a private interface just for _GeneratorContextManager.
See issue #11647 for details.

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._GeneratorContextManagerBase
documentation: Shared functionality for @contextmanager and @asynccontextmanager.

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._GeneratorContextManager
documentation: Helper for @contextmanager decorator.

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._AsyncGeneratorContextManager
documentation: Helper for @asynccontextmanager.

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.closing
documentation:
Context to automatically close something at the end of a block.

Code like this:

    with closing(<module>.open(<arguments>)) as f:
        <block>

is equivalent to this:

    f = <module>.open(<arguments>)
    try:
        <block>
    finally:
        f.close()

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.redirect_stdout
documentation:
Context manager for temporarily redirecting stdout to another file.

# How to send help() to stderr
with redirect_stdout(sys.stderr):
    help(dir)

# How to write help() to a file
with open('help.txt', 'w') as f:
    with redirect_stdout(f):
        help(pow)

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.redirect_stderr
documentation: Context manager for temporarily redirecting stderr to another file.

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.suppress
documentation:
Context manager to suppress specified exceptions

After the exception is suppressed, execution proceeds with the next
statement following the with statement.

     with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
         os.remove(somefile)
     # Execution still resumes here if the file was already removed

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._BaseExitStack

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._BaseExitStack.pop_all
documentation: Preserve the context stack by transferring it to a new instance.

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._BaseExitStack.push
documentation:
Registers a callback with the standard __exit__ method signature.

Can suppress exceptions the same way __exit__ method can.
Also accepts any object with an __exit__ method (registering a call
to the method instead of the object itself).

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._BaseExitStack.enter_context
documentation:
Enters the supplied context manager.

If successful, also pushes its __exit__ method as a callback and
returns the result of the __enter__ method.

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._BaseExitStack.callback
documentation:
Registers an arbitrary callback and arguments.

Cannot suppress exceptions.

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib._BaseExitStack._push_cm_exit
documentation: Helper to correctly register callbacks to __exit__ methods.
A base class for ExitStack and AsyncExitStack.

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.ExitStack

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.ExitStack.close
documentation: Immediately unwind the context stack.

Context manager for dynamic management of a stack of exit callbacks.

For example:
    with ExitStack() as stack:
        files = [stack.enter_context(open(fname)) for fname in filenames]
        # All opened files will automatically be closed at the end of
        # the with statement, even if attempts to open files later
        # in the list raise an exception.
Inspired by discussions on http://bugs.python.org/issue13585

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.AsyncExitStack

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.AsyncExitStack.enter_async_context
documentation:
Enters the supplied async context manager.

If successful, also pushes its __aexit__ method as a callback and
returns the result of the __aenter__ method.

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.AsyncExitStack.push_async_exit
documentation:
Registers a coroutine function with the standard __aexit__ method
signature.

Can suppress exceptions the same way __aexit__ method can.
Also accepts any object with an __aexit__ method (registering a call
to the method instead of the object itself).

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.AsyncExitStack.push_async_callback
documentation:
Registers an arbitrary coroutine function and arguments.

Cannot suppress exceptions.

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.AsyncExitStack.aclose
documentation: Immediately unwind the context stack.

methods.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.AsyncExitStack._push_async_cm_exit
documentation:
Helper to correctly register coroutine function to __aexit__
method.

Async context manager for dynamic management of a stack of exit
callbacks.

For example:
    async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
        connections = [await stack.enter_async_context(get_connection())
            for i in range(5)]
        # All opened connections will automatically be released at the
        # end of the async with statement, even if attempts to open a
        # connection later in the list raise an exception.
Inspired by discussions on https://bugs.python.org/issue29302

classes.name: vaex.jupyter.vendor.contextlib.nullcontext
documentation:
Context manager that does no additional processing.

Used as a stand-in for a normal context manager, when a particular
block of code is only sometimes used with a normal context manager:

cm = optional_cm if condition else nullcontext()
with cm:
    # Perform operation, using optional_cm if condition is True
